title: Backfill tooling for 2025Q1 unit economics aggregates (idempotent + throttled)
preview: Context: Finance needs QTD and quarter-close reporting in the internal Unit Economics dashboard, but the hourly aggregation job only covered data from the initial cutover onward. This PR adds an idempotent backfill tool to populate historical aggregates for 2025Q1 (and generally any arbitrary time window) while protecting the warehouse and avoiding double-counting. Scope: (1) Adds a backfill CLI (unit-econ-backfill) that replays the same aggregation logic as the hourly job over a time range; (2) Implements idempotency via per-hour partition overwrite semantics and a backfill_runs audit table; (3) Adds throttling (max concurrent partitions, adaptive sleep on warehouse pressure) and a maintenance-window mode; (4) Adds verification queries and a reconciliation helper view to compare pre/post backfill totals; (5) Updates runbook docs referenced by alerts. Idempotency model: the backfill operates on canonical hourly partitions (dt_hour UTC) in the materialized aggregate tables. For each dt_hour, it writes to a staging table and then atomically swaps/overwrites the target partition. A backfill_runs table records (run_id, requested_range, effective_partitions, rate_card_version, git_sha, started_at, completed_at, status) for auditability and for detecting partial runs. Concurrency + safety: default max_concurrency=4, with a --warehouse-safe flag that drops to 2 and increases sleep on query timeouts; --dry-run prints partition plan and estimated work; --resume uses backfill_runs to skip completed partitions; --force allows reprocessing (for rate card updates or logic changes) but still overwrites per-hour partitions to avoid duplication. Notes: We explicitly do not backfill raw metering events; we backfill derived aggregates only. This is intended for internal reporting and reconciliation and is gated behind internal authz. Testing: Added unit tests for partition planning/idempotency and integration tests against a local warehouse test container with seeded metering + rate card fixtures. Rollout: Run once for 2025-01-01..2025-03-31 (inclusive) in the analytics maintenance window; SRE will monitor warehouse saturation dashboards; Finance will validate QTD totals using the reconciliation view (linked below). Follow-ups: If Finance requests prior quarters, we can reuse the same tool; we may want a weekly scheduled backfill for late-arriving metering data, but that is out of scope for v1.

title: Fix throughput metric double-counting for streaming responses
preview:
Problem: Dedicated throughput SLO reporting was overcounting tokens for streaming requests. We were incrementing `deployment_tokens_generated_total` per-stream chunk (delta tokens) and then incrementing again on stream completion using the final `usage.completion_tokens` value. For non-streaming, the completion-path increment is correct; for streaming, it results in ~2x token totals (shape/model dependent) and skews derived tokens/sec in the SLO evaluator + dashboards.

Customer/ops impact: This primarily affects Dedicated throughput SLO compliance tracking (tokens/sec floor) and internal saturation indicators. It also contributed to customer confusion when comparing Console throughput graphs vs request-level `usage` totals (see SUP-18510). Billing exports are not impacted (billing uses metering events, not the SLO throughput counters).

Fix: Make streaming accounting single-source-of-truth by only emitting token increments from the streaming path, and skipping the completion-path increment when `streaming=true`. Additionally, guard against duplicate “final chunk” emission by tracking `last_emitted_completion_tokens` on the stream session and only emitting positive deltas.

What changed: - `api-gateway`: adjust token accounting so the completion handler does not increment throughput counters for streaming requests; the counter is incremented only via the stream chunk emitter. - `telemetry`: add explicit `streaming` + `accounting_source` labels to aid debugging (chunk vs completion). - tests: add unit tests for streaming lifecycle (N chunks + final usage) asserting exact token totals; add regression test covering the previously failing scenario where both chunk deltas and final usage were emitted.

Rollout notes: This changes the magnitude of streaming throughput counters immediately on deploy (expected ~-50% for affected traffic). SRE/Obs have been pinged to annotate the Dedicated throughput SLO dashboard and ignore pre/post comparisons for 24h.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): 1) Backfill/annotation for SLO reports that span the deploy window (SRE to decide). 2) Consider moving all throughput SLO computations to request-level usage events once metering latency is acceptable.

Validation: - Replayed a production trace set in staging (mixed streaming/non-streaming) and confirmed `deployment_tokens_generated_total` matches sum(usage.completion_tokens) within expected variance from retries/cancels. - Verified SLO evaluator output for a known Dedicated deployment no longer shows >2x spikes during high streaming traffic.

title: Add tests for customer tier mapping edge cases (dedicated/private flags, pilot credits)
preview: Context: We’ve had a few near-misses (and one minor backfill discrepancy) where `customer_tier` was derived differently depending on which upstream flags were present (dedicated routing metadata vs contract flags) and where some pilot/credit-only customers were incorrectly classified as enterprise. This PR adds targeted test coverage around tier derivation to prevent regressions as we continue to iterate on mapping rules for the Unit Economics dashboard. Changes: (1) Adds a comprehensive unit test matrix for tier derivation covering precedence rules between `dedicated`, `private`, `enterprise_contract`, `reseller`, and `pilot_credits`/`free_credits` flags. (2) Adds “golden” fixtures for known edge cases pulled from real anonymized records (IDs removed; synthetic account keys used). (3) Adds a few property-style tests to assert invariants (e.g., dedicated/private always win over self-serve; pilot credits never implicitly upgrade tier; unknown inputs map to `self_serve` + `tier_confidence=low`). (4) Extends backfill tooling tests to ensure historical rows don’t silently shift tiers when optional fields are missing. Notes/decisions encoded in tests: - Precedence: private > dedicated > enterprise > self_serve. - Reseller is treated as enterprise for v1 reporting but is emitted as `enterprise` with `tier_source=reseller_contract` (we don’t expose reseller as a separate tier yet). - `pilot_credits=true` does not upgrade a customer out of self-serve unless `enterprise_contract=true` (aligns with Finance methodology). - If both dedicated and private flags are set (shouldn’t happen, but has in older logs), we map to private and emit a warning metric (asserted in tests). Testing: `bazel test //analytics/...` and `//telemetry/...` plus added coverage run; see CI. Rollout: No prod behavior change expected (tests only), but this is a gate for future edits to tier mapping logic. Related: ENG-8423 (tier derivation) and ENG-8429 (data quality checks).

title: Bugfix: preserve rows when region mapping is null/unknown in unit economics aggregation
preview:
Context: After merging the region normalization work, we noticed intermittent drops in hourly unit economics aggregates where a small (but non-trivial) slice of metering rows had region unset or had a value that didn’t map (e.g. legacy colo labels, nulls from older gateway paths, or "unknown" emitted during partial outages). The aggregation job used an INNER JOIN to the canonical region mapping table, which caused those rows to be dropped entirely. This under-counted output_tokens and made $/1M output tokens look artificially worse/better depending on which spend inputs were present for that hour.

What this PR does: Update the unit economics aggregation query and the region mapping helper to be null-safe and non-dropping: (1) switch the join to LEFT JOIN, (2) COALESCE to a canonical region value of "unknown" when mapping fails, and (3) emit an explicit counter for unmapped rows so we can alert on drift instead of silently losing data. Also adds a small regression test covering null + unmapped region values.

Why this is safe: The change only affects how we assign the region dimension for rows that previously disappeared; mapped rows are unchanged. Downstream views already treat region as a dimension with a bounded value set, and adding "unknown" is within our cardinality guardrails.

Test plan: Run unit tests for the query builder + add a warehouse integration test fixture that includes null and unmapped region inputs; validate that output_tokens totals match pre-join totals and that the "unknown" bucket contains the expected rows.

Rollout: Deploy with the next scheduled analytics job release; monitor (a) unit_econ.region_mapping.unmapped_rows, (b) hourly completeness checks, and (c) reconciliation deltas vs billing totals. If the unmapped counter spikes, we’ll investigate upstream emitters rather than letting the aggregator hide the issue.

title: Backpressure controls + max lag safety (auto-pause) for downstream outages
preview:
Context: We had a beta incident where Splunk HEC throttling (429 + intermittent 503) caused exporter lag to spike and sustained retries increased queue pressure and CPU, impacting other tenants sharing the same exporter shard (see #incidents thread 1739559010 and SUP-18531 follow-up). This PR introduces explicit backpressure controls and a max-lag safety mechanism to keep the system stable during downstream outages while preserving at-least-once semantics.

What’s in this PR: (1) Max lag threshold per destination with auto-pause: if export_lag_seconds for a (tenant, destination) exceeds configurable max_lag_seconds for a sustained window, we stop pulling new batches for that destination and mark it PAUSED (reason=MAX_LAG) while continuing to process other destinations/tenants. (2) Backpressure based on in-flight bytes and retry backlog: introduce per-destination in_flight_bytes limits and a bounded retry queue; when exceeded we apply pull-side backoff rather than pushing more work into retries. (3) Circuit breaker integration for adapters: adapter-specific throttling signals (e.g., Splunk 429, Datadog 429/5xx) now feed a shared circuit breaker that increases backoff, and can trip into PAUSED when combined with lag growth. (4) Operator controls: new CLI commands to unpause a destination and to override max lag for controlled recovery. (5) Observability: new Prometheus metrics and structured log fields for pause/unpause transitions, reasons, and “time paused”; status endpoint now includes pause state and last_pause_reason.

Delivery semantics: No events are dropped. Auto-pause stops the exporter from advancing its checkpoint for the paused destination, so events remain available for replay/backfill once the downstream recovers and the destination is unpaused. This is consistent with our at-least-once contract; downstream dedup remains based on event_id.

Configuration: Adds exporter config keys (with safe defaults): max_lag_seconds (default 21600 / 6h), max_lag_grace_period_seconds (default 300), max_in_flight_bytes (default 64MiB), max_retry_queue_batches (default 500), pause_cooldown_seconds (default 900). All are feature-flagged behind BACKPRESSURE_V1 to limit beta blast radius.

Testing: unit tests for PauseController transitions; integration test with Splunk stub that returns sustained 429 and verifies: lag rises -> pause triggers -> pulling stops -> unpause resumes and drains; chaos-ish test ensures healthy destination continues despite one paused. Load test notes included in commit message, based on SRE backpressure experiments doc.

Rollout plan: Enable BACKPRESSURE_V1 for beta tenants only after deploying exporter + dashboards. Default max lag is conservative; SRE can tune per-tenant via config overrides if needed.

Checklist: [x] Metrics + dashboards hooks (exporter_paused, pause_reason, pause_duration_seconds) [x] Runbook updates TODO in separate PR (Sean requested) [x] Security review: no new PII; logs redact destination secrets [x] Feature flag gated (BACKPRESSURE_V1) [x] Integration tests cover 429/5xx.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): (a) surface pause/unpause controls in Console (control plane) (b) alerting thresholds tuning once we have 1-2 weeks of beta data (c) doc updates for customer-facing explanation of auto-pause + recovery expectations.

title: Audit event schema v2: add event_id + category taxonomy (compat mapping)
preview:
Context: As part of SIEM-ready audit export beta (ENG-5127), downstream delivery is at-least-once and we need stable dedup keys plus a consistent category taxonomy for filtering + mapping into Splunk/Datadog. This PR updates core audit event emission to include: (1) `event_id` (stable unique id per event) (2) `schema_version: 2` and canonical `category` / `subcategory` / `action` fields (3) a compatibility mapping layer so existing producers that still emit legacy `event_type`/`operation` continue to work while we progressively migrate call sites.

Summary of changes: Add `event_id` generated at emission time using UUIDv7 (time-ordered, collision-safe) and plumbed through the AuditEvent struct. Introduce an enum-backed category taxonomy with a small canonical set and a `category_version` constant to make future taxonomy evolution explicit. Add a `legacy_event_type` field (optional) and a mapping table from legacy strings to v2 taxonomy, used for any call site still emitting legacy types. Update JSON serialization + redaction hooks so `event_id` is always present and never redacted, while category fields are required and validated. Add golden fixtures + unit tests to lock schema output. No changes to retention/storage semantics in this PR.

Taxonomy (v2, initial): categories = {authn, authz, workspace, api_key, model, deployment, billing, data_access, security, system}. `subcategory` is optional but recommended for high-volume categories (e.g., `api_key.rotation`, `authz.role`, `deployment.route`). `action` remains a verb phrase in present tense (create/update/delete/rotate/enable/disable/login/logout/impersonate/etc) and is required.

Compatibility: Existing events that provide `event_type` will have `legacy_event_type` set, and the mapping table populates `category/subcategory/action`. If mapping is missing, we default to `category=system`, `subcategory=audit.unknown_legacy_type`, `action=emit` and include a structured warning log (rate-limited) so we can clean up unmapped call sites before GA.

Follow-ups: (a) migrate remaining emitters to pass v2 taxonomy explicitly, removing reliance on legacy mapping (b) update docs + examples in security/compliance Confluence (c) exporter service uses `event_id` for idempotency keys (separate PR).

Testing: unit tests for UUIDv7 generation + monotonicity (best-effort), mapping table tests (known legacy types), JSON golden files for representative events, and a redaction regression test to ensure `event_id/category/action` survive redaction.

Rollout: behind `AUDIT_SCHEMA_V2_ENABLED` (default ON in dev/staging, OFF in prod until exporter beta gate is ready). When flag is OFF, we still emit legacy fields only and omit v2 additions.

Risk: Low-medium. Primary risk is unexpected unmapped legacy event types causing `system/audit.unknown_legacy_type` categorization; mitigated via logs + metrics + follow-up cleanup.

title: Bootstrap audit exporter service skeleton + CI + local dev harness
preview:
## Summary
Bootstraps the new `audit-log-exporter` service repository with a minimal runnable skeleton, CI workflows, a base configuration model, and a local dev harness to support early iteration.

This is the first landing step for ENG-5139. No adapters, no delivery semantics, and no production wiring in this PR; those will follow in subsequent PRs.

## Motivation / Context
We need a dedicated service to read audit events (initially via stubbed source in local dev), apply tenant scoping, and deliver to downstream destinations with retries/checkpointing. Before we can iterate on architecture and adapters, we need:
- Standard project layout + build tooling
- Baseline CI (lint/test/build, container build)
- A config model (typed) that can evolve with control-plane config sync
- A local harness so devs can run the service end-to-end without needing the full control plane

## What’s included
- Service scaffold (Go)
  - `cmd/audit-exporter` main entrypoint
  - `internal/server` HTTP server with `/healthz` + `/readyz`
  - `internal/config` base config model + validation
  - `internal/logging` structured logging setup (json)
- CI / tooling
  - GitHub Actions for: `go test`, `go vet`, `golangci-lint`, `gofmt` check, and Docker build
  - Dependabot for Go modules + GitHub Actions
  - Makefile targets: `make test`, `make lint`, `make run`, `make docker-build`
- Local dev harness
  - `docker-compose.yml` to run exporter + a lightweight stub source (placeholder) + optional Prometheus
  - `./scripts/dev/generate_synthetic_events.sh` to emit sample audit events to stdout/file (temporary)
- Base config model (initial)
  - `listen_addr`, `log_level`
  - `tenant_id` (dev-only for now; will be replaced by multi-tenant config sync)
  - placeholder `export` section for future destinations (intentionally not wired)

## Not included (intentionally)
- No real event ingestion from Redwood services yet
- No destination adapters (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
- No checkpointing / delivery guarantees
- No filtering engine
- No secret management integration

## Testing
- Unit tests for config parsing + validation
- CI builds container image to ensure Dockerfile stays green
- Manual:
  - `make run` (starts HTTP server)
  - `docker compose up --build` (runs exporter + harness components)

## Follow-ups
- ENG-5132: Control plane export config API
- ENG-5144/5151/5156: Destination adapters
- ENG-5168: Metrics + tracing
- ENG-5189: Threat model items (egress allowlist hooks, secrets handling)

## Notes for reviewers
- Config model is deliberately minimal and will churn; goal is to establish patterns (validation, defaults, env overrides).
- Health endpoints are required for upcoming k8s deployment.

Closes: ENG-5139 (bootstrap portion)
text:
## Summary
Bootstraps the new `audit-log-exporter` service repository with a minimal runnable skeleton, CI workflows, a base configuration model, and a local dev harness to support early iteration.

This is the first landing step for ENG-5139. No adapters, no delivery semantics, and no production wiring in this PR; those will follow in subsequent PRs.

## Motivation / Context
We need a dedicated service to read audit events (initially via stubbed source in local dev), apply tenant scoping, and deliver to downstream destinations with retries/checkpointing. Before we can iterate on architecture and adapters, we need:
- Standard project layout + build tooling
- Baseline CI (lint/test/build, container build)
- A config model (typed) that can evolve with control-plane config sync
- A local harness so devs can run the service end-to-end without needing the full control plane

## What’s included
- Service scaffold (Go)
  - `cmd/audit-exporter` main entrypoint
  - `internal/server` HTTP server with `/healthz` + `/readyz`
  - `internal/config` base config model + validation
  - `internal/logging` structured logging setup (json)
- CI / tooling
  - GitHub Actions for: `go test`, `go vet`, `golangci-lint`, `gofmt` check, and Docker build
  - Dependabot for Go modules + GitHub Actions
  - Makefile targets: `make test`, `make lint`, `make run`, `make docker-build`
- Local dev harness
  - `docker-compose.yml` to run exporter + a lightweight stub source (placeholder) + optional Prometheus
  - `./scripts/dev/generate_synthetic_events.sh` to emit sample audit events to stdout/file (temporary)
- Base config model (initial)
  - `listen_addr`, `log_level`
  - `tenant_id` (dev-only for now; will be replaced by multi-tenant config sync)
  - placeholder `export` section for future destinations (intentionally not wired)

## Not included (intentionally)
- No real event ingestion from Redwood services yet
- No destination adapters (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
- No checkpointing / delivery guarantees
- No filtering engine
- No secret management integration

## Testing
- Unit tests for config parsing + validation
- CI builds container image to ensure Dockerfile stays green
- Manual:
  - `make run` (starts HTTP server)
  - `docker compose up --build` (runs exporter + harness components)

## Follow-ups
- ENG-5132: Control plane export config API
- ENG-5144/5151/5156: Destination adapters
- ENG-5168: Metrics + tracing
- ENG-5189: Threat model items (egress allowlist hooks, secrets handling)

## Notes for reviewers
- Config model is deliberately minimal and will churn; goal is to establish patterns (validation, defaults, env overrides).
- Health endpoints are required for upcoming k8s deployment.

Closes: ENG-5139 (bootstrap portion)

title: Add config sync from control plane (polling + streaming) with tenant scoping + caching
preview:
Implements config sync from the control plane for the audit exporter service. This adds (1) tenant scoping so a given exporter instance only processes configs it is authorized to see, (2) a resilient polling loop with jitter/backoff and a long-poll “watch” mode when supported by the control plane, and (3) an in-memory snapshot cache with safe defaults so we do not accidentally start exporting for a tenant unless the config is explicitly enabled.

Motivation
- ENG-5139 created the exporter scaffold; we need an end-to-end way to pull export destinations/filters from the control plane without requiring restarts.
- ENG-5132 is landing CRUD + validation in the control plane; this PR wires up the consumer side.

Key behaviors
- Startup: exporter boots in “no tenants enabled” state until it successfully fetches a config snapshot.
- Polling: periodic snapshot fetch (default 10s with +/-20% jitter) with exponential backoff on failures.
- Streaming/watch: if the control plane advertises watch support, we keep an open long-poll to get revision notifications and then refresh the snapshot (keeps overhead low while staying responsive).
- Tenant scoping: requests use an exporter service identity; control plane response is already filtered to authorized tenants. Exporter additionally enforces that it will only instantiate pipelines for tenants present in the snapshot and will tear down pipelines if a tenant disappears from the snapshot.
- Safe defaults: configs without destinations, without enabled=true, or with invalid/missing credential refs are treated as disabled and surfaced in health/status metrics (no export attempts).
- Caching: configs are stored as an atomic snapshot; adapters read from a read-only view to avoid lock contention.

Notes
- This PR intentionally does not fetch any secret material. Destination configs refer to secret references (e.g., vault/KMS-managed identifiers) that adapters will resolve through the existing secret provider interface.
- Filtering semantics are not implemented here (ENG-5160). We pass through filter blobs to keep the boundary stable.

Follow-ups
- Wire config revision + export status endpoint (lag/last error) once the exporter metrics PR lands.
- Add more “thundering herd” mitigation once we have multi-replica deployments (per-instance staggered start already included).

title: Add dashboards + alerts for data residency enforcement (blocked requests, policy eval latency)
preview: Summary: This PR adds a first-pass set of dashboards and alerting rules for Hosted data residency enforcement. The focus is on (1) visibility into enforcement outcomes (allowed vs blocked), (2) early detection of regressions in policy evaluation latency, and (3) detecting cross-region routing attempts that should be prevented once enforcement is enabled. Context: As part of Hosted data residency enforcement rollout (region pinning + deterministic request-time blocking), SRE and Security asked for a consistent set of metrics visualizations and actionable alerts. This pack is intended to be deployed alongside the routing/gateway changes and used during dogfood and progressive rollout. What’s included: Grafana dashboard: “Data Residency Enforcement” with panels: Overview: total requests, allowed/blocked, block-rate (%), and top block reasons (5m/1h). Blocked requests by region + endpoint: breakdown by edge region, model route, and request_type (streaming vs non-streaming). Blocked requests by org/project (top N): uses org_id/project_id labels when present; falls back to org_tier if IDs are intentionally redacted in some environments. Policy evaluation latency: p50/p95/p99 over time from residency policy decision histogram. Routing attempts: cross-region routing attempt counter and fallback suppression counter (to validate that fallback is being blocked unless explicitly allowed). Error contract adoption: panel showing residency_violation responses vs generic 5xx on streaming path (to catch the SUP-28577 class of issues). Alerts: 1) residency.block_rate_high (severity=page): triggers when blocked requests spike above expected baseline after filtering out known test orgs. Default threshold: > 2% block-rate AND > 30 blocked requests/min for 10m, grouped by region. 2) residency.block_rate_high_ticket (severity=ticket): triggers at lower thresholds to create early-warning noise without paging. Default threshold: > 0.5% AND > 10/min for 15m. 3) residency.policy_eval_latency_p95 (severity=page): p95 residency decision latency > 25ms for 10m, grouped by region (catches cache misses, policy service slowness, or routing layer regressions). 4) residency.cross_region_attempts (severity=page): any sustained non-zero cross-region routing attempt rate (> 1/min for 10m) after enforcement is enabled (gated via a deployment label so we don’t page during pre-enforcement dry runs). Example queries (PromQL) used in panels/alerts: Block rate (region): sum(rate(redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total{decision="blocked"}[5m])) by (region) / clamp_min(sum(rate(redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total[5m])) by (region), 1) Top block reasons: topk(10, sum(rate(redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total{decision="blocked"}[15m])) by (block_reason)) Policy eval p95 (region): histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(redwood_residency_policy_eval_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, region)) Cross-region attempts: sum(rate(redwood_residency_cross_region_routing_attempt_total[5m])) by (src_region, dst_region) Notes/assumptions: Metric names match the current instrumentation plan in routing/gateway branches: redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total (labels: decision, block_reason, region, endpoint, request_type, org_tier, org_id?, project_id?, policy_id?) redwood_residency_policy_eval_duration_seconds_bucket redwood_residency_cross_region_routing_attempt_total redwood_residency_fallback_suppressed_total If labels diverge slightly in final implementation, we can follow up with a small patch; this PR keeps queries isolated in jsonnet so updates are straightforward. Files changed (high level): dashboards/residency/enforcement-overview.jsonnet (new) dashboards/residency/enforcement-drilldowns.jsonnet (new) dashboards/residency/enforcement-errors.jsonnet (new) alerts/residency-enforcement.rules.yml (new) mixins/residency/README.md (new runbook links + definitions) dashboards/_index.jsonnet (register new dashboard) alerts/_index.yml (register new rules) Review notes: Sean Gallagher (SRE): Requested separating page vs ticket alerts and adding minimum volume gates to avoid paging during low-traffic regions. Addressed by adding dual alerts and clamp/volume checks; also added a panel for raw blocked count next to percentage. Rafael Mendes (SRE Manager): Asked for explicit links to runbooks and to tag alerts with the rollout phase label so we can disable paging during Phase 2 staging validation. Added runbook URLs and a required label residency_enforcement_enabled="true" to paging alerts where applicable. Logan Wright (Telemetry/audit): Confirmed that org_id/project_id labels are acceptable in internal metrics; requested a guard to allow redaction in some environments. Implemented by building panels that work with org_tier and optional org_id/project_id. Mina Haddad (Routing): Verified cross-region attempt panels align with how routing will emit src/dst labels; requested a panel to show fallback suppressed vs fallback attempted. Added a small comparison panel. Testing/verification: Rendered dashboards via jsonnet build locally; validated against staging Prometheus with synthetic counters from the residency canary job. Confirmed alert rules load successfully and evaluate with expected firing behavior when replaying a spike scenario. CI: lint-jsonnet: pass prometheus-rules-validate: pass dashboard-render-smoke: pass Outcome: Merged (squash) after approvals; safe to deploy with the next observability-pack rollout. Follow-ups: (1) Once gateway error contract is fully rolled out on streaming endpoints, we should tighten the “generic 5xx on streaming” panel and convert it into an alert for regressions (tracked in ENG-4833). (2) Consider adding an SLO panel (block-rate budget) per allowlisted enterprise org during Phase 3 rollout.

title: Add per-deployment throughput metrics (tokens/sec) + metric semantics doc
preview:
Context / Why: Dedicated tiering work needs a canonical, per-deployment throughput signal that SRE and Product can use for the throughput SLO. Today we only have aggregated tokens/sec at the cluster/pool level, which is not sufficient for customer-facing guarantees and makes triage hard when multiple deployments share infrastructure. ENG-19244 tracks adding a canonical per-deployment metric with consistent semantics across streaming/non-streaming and across routes (chat/completions). This PR adds the first version of those metrics (counters + derived rates), standard tags/labels, and initial documentation describing how to interpret them.

What’s in this PR: (1) New counters emitted at request completion for prompt/completion tokens, attributed to deployment_id and model, with route + region + tier tags. (2) A derived throughput panel-friendly series (tokens/sec) implemented as recording rules in our default Prometheus ruleset (so dashboards and SLO evaluator don’t have to re-encode queries). (3) Guardrails to keep label cardinality under control (deployment_id is only attached on Dedicated deployments; Hosted continues to roll up by route + model). (4) A short doc page that defines the metrics, label set, and the intended query patterns for SLO computation.

Metric details: New counters (monotonic) emitted from the API gateway on successful request completion (HTTP 2xx + 4xx where we have a complete usage payload; excludes transport aborts): redwood_inference_tokens_total{token_kind="prompt|completion", route="chat|completions", streaming="true|false", deployment_scope="dedicated|hosted", deployment_id, customer_id, tier, model, model_version, region}. A matching request counter is also emitted: redwood_inference_requests_total{route, streaming, deployment_scope, deployment_id, customer_id, tier, model, region, status_class="2xx|4xx|5xx"}. Throughput is intended to be computed as rate(redwood_inference_tokens_total[5m]) and/or a recording rule named redwood:throughput_tokens_per_sec:5m (see rules file). For Dedicated SLO use cases, the canonical query key is deployment_id; for non-Dedicated, use deployment_scope="hosted" and group by model/route/region.

Semantics / attribution: Tokens are sourced from the canonical usage object returned by the inference runtime (usage.prompt_tokens + usage.completion_tokens). For streaming responses, we still attribute tokens at end-of-request using the final usage summary (not per-chunk), which keeps the counter monotonic and avoids over-counting when clients disconnect/retry mid-stream. Requests that never reach a terminal usage summary (client disconnect before we receive a final usage event) will not increment tokens_total; those will be captured via request counters with status_class="5xx" or gateway error metrics, and are explicitly not part of throughput SLO numerator (follow-up doc in SLO definition PR).

Cardinality notes: deployment_id and customer_id are only set for Dedicated deployments (deployment_scope="dedicated"). For hosted traffic, these labels are omitted entirely (not empty string) to avoid high-cardinality explosions. model_version is only populated when the caller pins a model version (or the runtime returns a resolved revision); otherwise it is omitted. tier is populated for Dedicated only and reflects the control-plane tier assignment at request admission time.

Docs: Adds docs/observability/metrics/dedicated-throughput.md describing: metric names, label set, how to compute tokens/sec, what not to do (don’t group by request_id; don’t treat tokens_total as billed tokens; don’t compare across models without normalization), and example PromQL snippets used by dashboards.

Testing / validation: Unit tests cover label suppression for hosted traffic, correct prompt/completion increments, and basic recording rule evaluation using golden test vectors. Added an integration test in api-gateway that asserts a streaming request produces exactly one counter increment at completion and includes deployment_id only when the request is routed to a Dedicated deployment.

Follow-ups: (a) Wire the new recording rule into the Dedicated SLO evaluator (tracked in ENG-19288 / SLO toolkit PR). (b) Add an explicit metric for “incomplete usage / client abort” so we can reason about missing token attribution during incidents. (c) Extend docs with an SLO-oriented cookbook once the tier exclusions are finalized.

Risk: Low to medium. This adds new metric emission in the gateway request completion path; emission is async and bounded, but please watch telemetry pipeline backpressure. We’ve feature-flagged deployment_id tagging behind telemetry.deployment_attribution.enabled (default on for Dedicated clusters, off for hosted). Rollout plan is to enable in one Dedicated region (us-east) for 48 hours, then expand.

Screenshots: N/A (metrics only).

Refs: Linear ENG-19244. Related Dedicated throughput SLO workstream: PM-482 / SRE SLO definition doc (separate PR).

title: Add audit log events for residency policy create/update/delete + wire to audit-log-exporter
preview: Context: As part of Hosted data residency enforcement we need auditable change history for residency pinning configuration (org/project/api-key level). This PR emits audit log events on residency policy mutations (create/update/delete) and wires those events into the existing audit-log-exporter pipeline so they are available in Console audit log views and customer exports. Scope: Control plane (residency policy service) emits events; exporter normalizes/ships; no changes to request-time enforcement here. Event types (v1): residency_policy.created, residency_policy.updated, residency_policy.deleted, residency_policy.override_applied (admin-only, gated behind flag). Payload highlights: actor (user_id/service, auth_type, ip where available), target (org_id, project_id nullable, api_key_id nullable), policy_id, allowed_regions, primary_region, allow_cross_region_fallback, effective_from, previous_values (for updates, limited), request_id/trace_id for correlation, and metadata (event_version=1, source=control_plane, environment). Security constraints: do NOT include any customer prompt/content; for api_key identifiers we include api_key_id (internal UUID) and last4 only; we do not emit raw key material. For update events, previous_values are limited to residency-related fields only and exclude any free-form notes/description fields. Exporter notes: events are routed to the same destination as other config/audit events; this is metadata-only and allowed per ADR-021 boundaries. Testing: unit tests for event emission on CRUD, exporter schema validation tests, and an integration test that asserts event appears in audit-log-exporter queue with expected fields. Rollout: guarded by feature flag residency_audit_events_v1 (default off); can be enabled per environment. Follow-ups: Console UI will consume these events in DES-611 work; docs to be updated in redwood-docs PR-1189. Checklist: - [x] Emit events on create/update/delete - [x] Add admin override event (flagged) - [x] Add exporter mapping + schema validation - [x] Add tests - [x] Security review sign-off - [x] SRE review for exporter volume impact

title: Add Datadog Logs destination adapter + audit tag/facet mapping
preview: Summary: This PR adds a Datadog Logs destination adapter to the audit-log-exporter and establishes a stable mapping from audit event schema v2 -> Datadog tags/facets. It also introduces rate-limit aware batching behavior (primarily for 429/503) so we degrade gracefully under Datadog intake constraints while preserving at-least-once semantics. Context: As part of the SIEM-ready audit export beta, we support Splunk HEC, S3, and Datadog Logs. Datadog differs from Splunk in that (a) intake is strongly rate-limited, and (b) the best user experience is via consistent tagging/faceting for filtering/dashboards in Datadog. This PR aligns with ENG-5156 and the delivery semantics ADR (at-least-once, downstream dedup via event_id). Design notes (high-level): Adapter: Adds `datadog_logs` destination type with `site`, `intake_path`, and `api_key_secret_ref`. We send batches to `https://http-intake.logs.{site}/api/v2/logs` (default) with JSON payloads. Each audit event becomes one log item with a message, attributes, and tags. Tag/facet mapping: We map audit event fields into a consistent set of Datadog tags for filtering. Primary tags: `redwood.tenant_id`, `redwood.workspace_id`, `redwood.category`, `redwood.action`, `redwood.resource_type`, `redwood.actor_type`, and optionally `redwood.actor_id` (only when allowed by audit policy). We also set `service=redwood-audit-export`, `ddsource=redwood`, and `env=<region|deployment>` (exporter runtime env). We keep the raw payload minimal: we do not include request bodies, prompt text, or free-form metadata; event context is limited to schema v2 allowed fields and pre-existing redactions. Rate-limit aware batching: The adapter maintains a per-destination adaptive batch size. On 429, we back off with jitter and reduce batch size (floor 1). On success, we slowly increase toward the configured max (AIMD-like behavior) to recover throughput without oscillation. We also respect `Retry-After` when present and otherwise default to exponential backoff using the exporter retry policy. Checkpointing/dedup: We do not attempt exactly-once delivery. Each log item includes `event_id` as `redwood.event_id` (tag + attribute) so customers can dedup. We preserve ordering within a partition best-effort but do not guarantee strict order under retries. Security/PII: The adapter redacts sensitive fields in logs and avoids logging payload contents. Exporter logs include only: destination id, batch size, response status, dd request id (when provided), and retry wait. API key is loaded via secret ref; no key material in config logs. Tests: Adds unit tests for tag mapping (category->tag), and integration-ish tests via stubbed intake server covering 200, 429 with Retry-After, and 503 transient errors. Operators: Adds additional metrics for adapter status codes and rate-limit backoff behavior to help SRE tune defaults. Rollout: Feature-flagged behind destination validation at control plane; exporter supports the destination type when present in config sync. Follow-ups: (1) support additional Datadog intake paths for Gov/US3 sites (config already allows override); (2) add customer-facing docs and a Console “Test connection” for Datadog (tracked elsewhere).

title: Add Splunk HEC destination adapter (gzip compression, 429 backoff, per-token circuit breaker)
preview: Summary: Implements a new Splunk HEC destination adapter for SIEM-ready audit exports. The adapter supports gzip-compressed payloads, robust handling of downstream throttling (429) with Retry-After-aware exponential backoff + jitter, and a per-token circuit breaker to avoid hot-looping on bad credentials or persistent throttling. Motivation: Beta customers are primarily Splunk-first and we observed exporter lag spikes when HEC returns sustained 429s; this change improves delivery stability and reduces noisy retry behavior. Scope: Adapter implementation + config schema + basic unit tests + metrics hooks. Not included: Splunk indexer acknowledgment (HEC ACK) support (will track separately), advanced event field remapping beyond the audit schema’s SIEM mapping guidance. Delivery semantics: remains at-least-once; downstream Splunk consumers should dedupe on event_id if needed. Config: adds splunk_hec destination type with endpoint_url, token_ref, index/source/sourcetype (optional), max_batch_events/max_batch_bytes, flush_interval, request_timeout, max_retries, circuit_breaker thresholds and cooldown. Security: token is read via secret ref only; adapter logs redact token and never log full request bodies; destination validation ensures https and blocks loopback/private ranges when egress allowlist is enabled. Observability: adds adapter-level counters for requests, bytes, response codes, retries, breaker_open, and last_success_timestamp; integrates into existing exporter metrics namespace. Test plan: unit tests for batching, gzip encoding, retry/backoff timing (using fake clock), circuit breaker transitions; local manual validation against Splunk HEC sandbox container with artificial 429 injection. Checklist: [x] unit tests added/updated [x] lint/format [x] docs updated for destination config [x] metrics added [ ] e2e harness coverage (follow-up in ENG-5195).

title: Add dead-letter queue + guarded replay CLI for failed export batches
preview: Summary: This PR introduces a dead-letter queue (DLQ) for audit export batches that fail delivery after exhausting retries, and adds an operator-facing replay tool with guardrails. This is a reliability/safety feature for the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC / Datadog Logs / S3) to ensure we never silently drop events while still protecting the platform and customers from accidental replay storms. Context / Motivation: During downstream outages (e.g., sustained 429s from Splunk HEC or 403s from mis-scoped Datadog keys), the exporter currently retries with backoff and then surfaces a per-destination error. Without DLQ persistence we risk either (a) losing batches on process/queue churn or (b) having no safe operational path to replay specific failures once a customer fixes credentials/quotas. ENG-5164 calls for a DLQ + replay tooling with explicit operator controls. Design highlights: (1) DLQ record model: When a batch hits max_delivery_attempts OR we detect a non-retryable adapter error, we persist a DLQ record keyed by (tenant_id, export_config_id, checkpoint_range) and store the batch payload (newline-delimited JSON events) in object storage under a structured prefix. DLQ record includes: dlq_id, created_at, tenant_id, export_config_id, destination_type, reason_code, reason_detail (redacted), first_event_ts, last_event_ts, event_count, byte_size, last_attempt_at, attempt_count, and a payload_ref. (2) At-least-once compatibility: Replays may cause duplicate deliveries. We intentionally do not attempt exactly-once; downstream should dedup using event_id (per ADR on delivery semantics). Replay CLI logs a reminder and requires an explicit --ack-duplicates flag. (3) Operator replay CLI: Adds bin/audit-exporter-replay (Go) to list DLQ items and replay in a controlled fashion. Guardrails include: required --tenant-id and --export-config-id; default is dry-run; interactive confirmation unless --yes is provided; bounded selection via --since/--until and --max-items; token-bucket rate limiting (--max-events-per-minute) to avoid spiking adapters; refuses to replay if export config is disabled OR if exporter feature flag is off for that tenant (unless --override-feature-flag is provided, which is restricted to on-call breakglass role). (4) Safety checks: Replay validates destination configuration hash matches the DLQ record by default (prevents replaying old payloads into a newly re-pointed sink). Can be overridden with --ignore-config-hash-mismatch for incident response, but emits a prominent warning. (5) Redaction: DLQ reason_detail is run through the same redaction helper used by adapter logs to avoid accidentally persisting tokens/keys/PII. Payloads contain audit events only (already subject to audit schema/PII policy); we do not include request/response bodies. (6) Observability: Adds metrics: audit_exporter_dlq_enqueued_total{tenant_id_hash,destination_type,reason_code}, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog_items{destination_type}, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog_events{destination_type}, audit_exporter_dlq_replay_total{result}, and audit_exporter_dlq_replay_latency_seconds. Also adds structured logs for DLQ enqueue and replay runs (with correlation_id). User-visible / Ops workflow: - When an adapter can’t deliver a batch and we exceed retry limits, exporter transitions the batch to DLQ and advances the live pipeline checkpoint to prevent system-wide backpressure. - On-call or Support can instruct customer to fix auth/limits, then run replay for the impacted tenant/config/time window. - Replay is incremental and rate-limited; any failures during replay are re-DLQ’d with a new reason_code (replay_failed_*). Example commands: - List backlog: ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq list --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --until 2025-02-09T06:00:00Z - Dry-run replay (default): ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq replay --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --max-items 10 - Execute replay with explicit acknowledgements: ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq replay --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --max-items 50 --max-events-per-minute 12000 --ack-duplicates --yes Testing: - Unit tests for DLQ persistence, reason_code mapping, and redaction - Integration tests using adapter stubs: force 429/403/non-retryable to verify DLQ enqueue and payload roundtrip - Manual: replayed a synthetic backlog into Splunk stub with rate limiting and verified dedup by event_id Related: - Linear: ENG-5164 - ADR: delivery semantics and idempotency (at-least-once + event_id) - Support context: SUP-18531 (perceived missing events during throttling) Notes / Follow-ups: - Dashboard panels/alerts for DLQ backlog are tracked separately (we add metrics here, panels in observability-pack). - For beta, replay CLI is intended for on-call / SRE / Support escalation only; we are not exposing self-serve replay in Console.

title: Unit economics: add reconciliation report vs billing totals (daily + monthly)
preview: Adds a reconciliation report and warehouse view that compares the Unit Economics pipeline aggregates (blended $/1M output tokens and underlying $ + tokens) against billing totals for the same time window. This is intended as a Finance sign-off tool and a recurring data quality guardrail for exec reporting (QTD/MTD). The report is internal-only and intentionally conservative about what it claims to reconcile: we reconcile compute-related cost attribution and token-metered usage to billing line items that are attributable to inference usage; we explicitly exclude taxes, one-time fees, manual adjustments, and contract-specific true-ups that are not represented in metering. Implementation overview: (1) introduces a canonical reconciliation window definition (UTC by default, with a documented option to run in Finance reporting TZ), (2) materializes daily and monthly reconciliation tables keyed by customer_tier + region + model_family (optional drilldown) and a top-level rollup, (3) implements tolerance-based pass/fail gates with reason codes (e.g., missing metering hours, rate-card version mismatch, billing lag), and (4) wires a lightweight CLI + scheduled job to publish a daily snapshot and a CSV export for FP&A. Guardrails: we hard-fail the report if metering completeness is below threshold (see econ_pipeline_completeness_pct), if the billing extract is missing the last N hours for the requested window, or if the GPU rate card version differs from the one pinned for the window; we soft-warn for low-volume segments where rounding dominates. Known limitations (documented in the report header and README): refunds/credits are applied at invoice time and may land outside the usage window; dedicated/private contracts often bill at committed capacity rather than metered tokens (we still provide a reconciliation section, but it is labeled "not expected to match" unless contract flags indicate usage-based billing); resellers can aggregate billing across multiple internal org_ids; and streaming output token parity issues can bias the token side if a regression occurs (this report surfaces the discrepancy but does not correct it). Testing: added unit tests for billing line-item classification, window alignment, tolerance math, and reason-code prioritization; added an end-to-end test with a fixture billing export and a fixture metering aggregate for a known-good day. Rollout: behind internal feature flag UNIT_ECON_RECONCILIATION_REPORT; Finance (Laura/Rishi) requested this as a go/no-go artifact for v1; once merged we will run in shadow mode for 1 week and tune tolerances before gating the exec KPI pack.

title: Implement S3 destination adapter (batching, partitioning, checkpoints, retries)
preview:
Context: This PR adds the first destination adapter for the SIEM-ready audit export beta: S3 delivery. The adapter is built around (a) deterministic partitioning for cost/operability, (b) bounded batching for throughput, and (c) at-least-once delivery via checkpoint persistence.

Linear: ENG-5144

What’s included:
- New S3 destination adapter implementing the exporter Destination interface
- Batching policy: flush on max_events, max_bytes, or flush_interval (defaults tuned for beta)
- Partitioning scheme (configurable prefix + time partitioning + tenant scoping) to avoid hot prefixes and keep search-friendly layouts
- Checkpoint persistence for at-least-once semantics (write object -> persist checkpoint). Cursor is (emitted_at, event_id) and is treated as an opaque ordering token by downstream
- Retry/backoff for S3 throttling/transient errors (SlowDown/503/timeouts) with jitter and a max retry cap; non-retryable errors go to DLQ (DLQ plumbing is stubbed behind an interface; full operator tooling comes in ENG-5164)
- Minimal metrics around adapter throughput, batch size, upload latency, and retry counts
- Unit tests for batching edge cases (size boundaries, timer flush, retry classification) and a small local dev harness doc

Out of scope (follow-ups):
- SSE-KMS + cross-account assume role (tracked in ENG-5148; implemented in PR #1068)
- Integration tests with real AWS / localstack matrix (tracked in ENG-5195)
- Console UI + config CRUD (control-plane work)

Implementation details:
- Object format: newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON) where each line is a single canonical audit event payload (already emitted by upstream as JSON). We intentionally avoid wrapping in a top-level array to support streaming/partial reads in customer tooling.
- Object key layout (default): s3://<bucket>/<prefix>/tenant_id=<tenant>/workspace_id=<workspace>/dt=YYYY-MM-DD/hour=HH/<destination_id>_<unix_ms>_<batch_seq>.ndjson
  - tenant_id/workspace_id are included to make customer-side permissions/audits easier when exporting multiple workspaces into a shared bucket.
  - dt/hour partitions keep file counts reasonable for typical SIEM collectors.
- Checkpoint store: uses the exporter’s checkpoint repository abstraction backed by the existing service DB (per-tenant scoped). Each destination has a single monotonically advancing cursor.
  - We only advance the checkpoint after the upload succeeds.
  - If checkpoint update fails after upload, we will re-upload on restart (duplicates are expected; downstream should dedup on event_id).
- Retry policy: exponential backoff with decorrelated jitter. We treat 429/SlowDown/5xx/timeouts as retryable. 403/AccessDenied and 400/BadRequest are non-retryable and are surfaced as destination health errors.
- Guardrails: adapter enforces a max object size (hard cap) and will split batches accordingly; also validates bucket + prefix format early so we fail fast during config validation.

Operational notes:
- Adapter emits structured logs with destination_id/tenant_id/workspace_id, but never logs event payloads.
- Added a per-destination circuit-breaker-ish counter (consecutive failures) used by the exporter loop to mark destination unhealthy (full auto-pausing is in PR #1119).

Testing:
- Unit tests for batching and retry classification (go test ./...)
- Manual smoke test using AWS dev bucket documented in docs/dev/s3-smoke-test.md

Rollout:
- No runtime behavior change unless S3 destination type is configured. Feature flag gating is handled upstream in control-plane; this PR is safe to deploy to exporter environments ahead of time.

  - tenant_id/workspace_id are included to make customer-side permissions/audits easier when exporting multiple workspaces into a shared bucket.
  - dt/hour partitions keep file counts reasonable for typical SIEM collectors.
- Checkpoint store: uses the exporter’s checkpoint repository abstraction backed by the existing service DB (per-tenant scoped). Each destination has a single monotonically advancing cursor.
  - We only advance the checkpoint after the upload succeeds.
  - If checkpoint update fails after upload, we will re-upload on restart (duplicates are expected; downstream should dedup on event_id).
- Retry policy: exponential backoff with decorrelated jitter. We treat 429/SlowDown/5xx/timeouts as retryable. 403/AccessDenied and 400/BadRequest are non-retryable and are surfaced as destination health errors.
- Guardrails: adapter enforces a max object size (hard cap) and will split batches accordingly; also validates bucket + prefix format early so we fail fast during config validation.

Operational notes:
- Adapter emits structured logs with destination_id/tenant_id/workspace_id, but never logs event payloads.
- Added a per-destination circuit-breaker-ish counter (consecutive failures) used by the exporter loop to mark destination unhealthy (full auto-pausing is in PR #1119).

Testing:
- Unit tests for batching and retry classification (go test ./...)
- Manual smoke test using AWS dev bucket documented in docs/dev/s3-smoke-test.md

Rollout:
- No runtime behavior change unless S3 destination type is configured. Feature flag gating is handled upstream in control-plane; this PR is safe to deploy to exporter environments ahead of time.

title: Add quantization_profile to request metadata + metering (with allowed-value enforcement)
preview:
Context

Unit Economics dashboard v1 needs a stable dimension for grouping cost/1M output tokens by quantization profile (fp16 vs int8 vs 4-bit, etc). We currently have model/version, region, tier, and streaming flag in metering; quantization profile is only implicitly encoded in some model ids and can’t be reliably joined in analytics. This PR adds an explicit quantization_profile tag to request metadata and ensures it’s emitted to metering with cardinality guardrails.

What this PR does

- Adds quantization_profile to the request metadata object that flows from gateway -> runtime -> metering.
- Emits quantization_profile as a first-class metering dimension for token usage (input/output) and request-level latency metrics.
- Enforces allowed values at the gateway boundary to prevent high-cardinality or free-form strings from polluting metrics. Unknown/unset is normalized to "unknown".
- Adds a small mapping helper for runtime-side quantization configs to map to the canonical string values used in telemetry.
- Adds dashboards/alerts-friendly annotations (metric docs + sample queries) for Analytics to validate coverage.

Allowed values (v1)

We intentionally keep this small and versioned:
- "unknown" (default)
- "fp16"
- "bf16"
- "int8"
- "awq-4bit"
- "gptq-4bit"
- "nf4"

If a request includes an unrecognized value, we either: (a) reject for internal callers that set the field explicitly (400 with message listing allowed values), or (b) normalize to "unknown" when the field is absent. External/public API does not expose this field yet; it’s currently only set by internal routing/model selection.

Implementation notes

- Gateway: validates request_context.quantization_profile against a compiled allowlist and injects it into the internal request context that is forwarded to runtime.
- Runtime: when a model is loaded with a quantization config, it sets quantization_profile on the request span/context if absent (so we don’t rely solely on gateway injection for internal paths).
- Telemetry: the token metering event includes quantization_profile as a dimension. We keep it at request scope (not per-token) and reuse for both streaming and non-streaming emission paths.
- Cardinality: quantization_profile is explicitly included in the metering dimension schema and guardrails treat it as an enum dimension (hard cap == allowlist). This is aligned with ENG-8432.

Changed areas (high level)

- api-gateway/
  - request context schema + validation
  - forwarding headers/metadata (internal)
- serving-runtime/
  - request context propagation
  - quant config -> canonical profile mapping helper
- observability/
  - metering event schema updates
  - dimension registry update + guardrails
  - tests validating tag emission and normalization

Illustrative snippet (gateway validation)

- Added a centralized enum:
  const ALLOWED_QUANT_PROFILES = new Set(["unknown","fp16","bf16","int8","awq-4bit","gptq-4bit","nf4"]);
- Validation behavior:
  - if provided and not in allowlist => 400 (internal only)
  - if missing/empty => "unknown"

Test plan

- Unit tests:
  - validates allowlist enforcement and "unknown" normalization
  - validates that runtime-side mapping never emits a value outside allowlist
- Integration tests:
  - non-streaming path: metering emits quantization_profile
  - streaming path: metering emits quantization_profile (ensures parity)
- Metrics sanity:
  - verified locally that the dimension registry accepts the new tag and does not increase cardinality budget

Rollout

- Behind internal feature flag: unit_econ_quant_profile_dim
- Staged rollout:
  1) enable in staging for metering only
  2) enable in one prod region for internal traffic
  3) expand to all regions once coverage is >99% non-unknown for supported models

Follow-ups

- Wire quantization_profile into the Unit Economics aggregation job (ENG-8426) once metering coverage is stable.
- Consider surfacing quantization_profile in internal model catalog metadata to avoid duplicating mapping logic.

Notes for Analytics/Finance

- Expect a temporary skew toward "unknown" while older runtimes are still deployed; this should decrease as the runtime mapping path rolls out.
- Reconciliation should treat "unknown" as a valid bucket (do not drop rows).

title: Derive customer_tier dimension for unit economics + backfill tooling
preview:
Context: Unit Economics dashboard needs a finance-aligned, stable `customer_tier` dimension to support drilldowns and reconciliation. Today tier is implicitly inferred ad-hoc in queries (billing plan here, dedicated tags there), which creates mismatches and makes backfills painful.

This PR introduces a canonical derivation path for `customer_tier` and a safe backfill tool to populate the dimension for historical metering events used by the aggregation job.

What this PR does: 1) Adds a canonical `customer_tier` derivation function used in the metering/analytics ingestion path. Output values are a controlled enum: `self_serve | dedicated | private | enterprise | unknown`. 2) Establishes the source-of-truth precedence order (documented in code + comments) so we get deterministic outcomes across services: (a) Explicit contract metadata (enterprise/private flags) from Billing/Contracts snapshot (daily) (b) Dedicated/private routing signals from gateway/runtime (request metadata) (c) Billing plan / account type for self-serve (d) Fallback to `unknown` with a counter + sampling log.

Edge cases handled: - Resellers / channel: map to `enterprise` when `contract.channel_partner_id` is present and account is marked `invoice_billing=true`; otherwise `unknown` until Contracts snapshot has the mapping (we emit a warning metric so we can track this). - Pilots/POCs/credits: if `billing.plan=free_credits` but `contract.enterprise=true`, tier is `enterprise` (finance wants tier to reflect GTM motion/infra shape, not temporary credits). - Dedicated but billed under enterprise master agreement: dedicated routing wins (`dedicated`), since cost attribution differs (reserved pools) even if contract is enterprise. - Private deployments with shared control plane: tier is `private` if `routing.deployment_mode=private` OR `contract.private=true`; avoids mislabeling private traffic as enterprise hosted.

Backfill tooling: - Adds `tools/unit_econ/backfill_customer_tier` which replays metering rows for a time window, joins on the Contracts snapshot effective at event_time (with a 24h grace window), and writes the derived tier into the `metering_events_v2.customer_tier` column (or the sidecar dimension table when column is absent; see migration notes below). - The job is idempotent and chunked by hour partitions. It supports `--dry-run`, `--limit-hours`, and `--throttle-ms` to avoid overloading the warehouse. - Includes verification queries printed at the end (coverage %, unknown %, tier distribution deltas) for oncall/finance signoff.

Schema/migrations: - Adds `customer_tier` to the normalized dimension set in metering v2 (nullable during rollout). - Adds a compact mapping table `dim_customer_tier_source` for debugging (why a row got a tier) without exploding cardinality; values are `contract | routing | billing | fallback`.

Rollout plan: - Step 1 (this PR): derive tier on write + backfill tool behind feature flag `unit_econ_customer_tier_derivation`. - Step 2: run backfill for 2025-01-01..present during off-peak; monitor warehouse load + unknown rate. - Step 3: turn on the aggregation job to require non-null tier (with `unknown` allowed), and update dashboard queries.

Notes for reviewers: Finance cares most about deterministic mapping + reconciliation stability. SRE cares about backfill safety and not breaking ingestion when upstream contract snapshot lags. Please focus review on precedence rules and failure modes.

Screens/validation: - Staging: ran backfill for a 6-hour window; unknown rate 0.7% (expected, mostly reseller accounts missing contract linkage). - Verified dedicated traffic is correctly labeled via gateway `dedicated_pool_id` for internal dogfood accounts.

Follow-ups: - Add reseller contract linkage to Contracts snapshot (separate work item). - Once stable, remove legacy query-side tier inference in `warehouse/views/unit_econ_*`.

title: Add hourly unit economics aggregation job + warehouse schema/materialized views
preview: Context: This PR implements the v1 hourly aggregation job for the internal Unit Economics dashboard. It introduces warehouse schema migrations, a scheduled aggregator (hourly) that joins token metering with cost inputs (GPU rate card + allocation factors), and initial materialized views to power cost/$ per 1M output tokens drilldowns by model, region, and customer tier. This is the first production path for the canonical metric (blended $/1M output tokens) and is intended to be finance-aligned while still allowing iteration via versioned logic. Scope: (1) New warehouse tables for hourly aggregates and reconciliation-friendly rollups, (2) A job that runs hourly with late-arrival handling and idempotent upserts, (3) Materialized views for dashboard queries (time series + top-N breakdowns), (4) Data validation checks to fail fast on missing dimensions and prevent silent drops. Non-goals: Streaming output token parity is handled in a separate PR; this assumes output_tokens is present and accurate in metering. Implementation details: The aggregator reads from `metering.request_tokens_hourly` (or equivalent staged fact) and joins against `finance.gpu_rate_card_hourly` via normalized region + provider + capacity_type. We allocate shared/idle components using the current v1 allocation factors table (owned by Finance), then compute blended cost per output token. All cost computations are stored in USD with 6 decimals; dashboard views scale to $/1M output tokens. Key tables: `analytics.unit_econ_hourly_fact_v1` (grain: hour x model_id x model_version x quant_profile x region_norm x customer_tier), `analytics.unit_econ_hourly_rollup_v1` (grain: hour x region_norm x customer_tier; used for reconciliation), `analytics.unit_econ_job_runs` (job run audit / late data tracking). Key MVs: `analytics.mv_unit_econ_ts_by_model_region_tier_v1` (time series), `analytics.mv_unit_econ_topn_models_7d_v1`, `analytics.mv_unit_econ_topn_regions_7d_v1`, `analytics.mv_unit_econ_topn_tiers_7d_v1`. Idempotency / late data: We reprocess a sliding window (default 36h) each run to incorporate late-arriving metering, then upsert by primary key (hour + dims + version). We also persist `processed_through_hour` and row counts per run for oncall visibility. Performance: Hourly fact table is partitioned by day(hour_ts) and clustered by (region_norm, model_id, customer_tier). MVs use pre-aggregated measures to keep dashboard query latency <2s for common groupings. Illustrative snippet (aggregation core): `blended_cost_usd = (gpu_compute_cost_usd + gpu_idle_alloc_usd + shared_overhead_alloc_usd)` and `blended_usd_per_1m_output_tokens = (blended_cost_usd / nullif(output_tokens,0)) * 1e6`. Validation: The job asserts non-null for required dims (model_id, region_norm, customer_tier) and records a `dropped_rows_reason` metric when input facts are excluded (e.g., unknown region mapping). Follow-ups: (a) streaming output token parity (ENG-8421), (b) region normalization table productionized (ENG-8424), (c) quantization_profile tagging (ENG-8425). Test plan: `bin/unit_econ_agg --dry-run --start 2025-01-28T00:00Z --end 2025-01-28T06:00Z` against staging; compare rollup totals to billing exports for the same window (within tolerance).

title: Fix streaming output token metering parity + add regression tests
preview: Context: We found a consistent under-count of output tokens for streaming chat/completions compared to the non-streaming path, which makes blended $/1M output tokens look artificially high for streaming-heavy workloads and breaks parity in the unit economics aggregation. Root cause: the streaming emitter was incrementing output_tokens from partial deltas, but skipped accounting for (a) tool/function-call argument tokens in streamed tool calls, and (b) the final “finish_reason” frame when the last delta was empty. In addition, the truncation path (max_tokens / server-side truncation) could exit early without emitting the final accounting event. Changes in this PR: (1) Move streaming output token accounting to a single authoritative counter that is updated from the same tokenization boundary used for billing/metering (token_count_v2) rather than from per-chunk heuristics. (2) Ensure we emit exactly one metering event per request with finalized counts (output_tokens, input_tokens, total_tokens) regardless of early exits, cancellations, or truncation. (3) Add a streaming/non-streaming parity test suite that replays recorded streaming transcripts (including tool calls) and asserts output token equivalence within 0 drift (exact match). (4) Add metric parity validation in canary mode: for 1% of traffic, compute output tokens via both codepaths and emit a debug gauge of diff; alerting will be handled in ENG-8429 but this PR includes the metric emission and sampling gate. Safety/compatibility: No API surface changes. Metering event schema unchanged; only counts become correct. This will change historical time series in dashboards going forward (no backfill in this PR). Rollout: ship behind feature flag metering.streaming_output_token_fix; enable in staging immediately; ramp prod 0%->10%->50%->100% over 24h while monitoring token_diff and billing reconciliation deltas. Notes: This is intentionally scoped as a hotfix to restore parity; follow-ups for retro/backfill and documentation are tracked in the unit economics workstream.
